title: To amend the Convention on Cultural Property Implementation Act to make certain technical corrections to facilitate the lawful trade and collecting of numismatic materials.
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for bringing old coins, medals, and paper money into the United States. Right now, customs officers can ask for lots of different paperwork to prove these items can legally enter the country. The new law would make it easier by saying people only need to show that their coins or money were bought legally, are a known type, and were not stolen from archaeological sites. Customs officers could only ask for extra proof if they think the paperwork is fake.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

sections.title: Section 1  Amendments to Convention on Cultural Property Implementation Act
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Convention on Cultural Property Implementation Act. The law currently has rules about bringing cultural items into the United States from other countries. First, the section adds a new definition. It defines numismatic material to include coins, tokens, paper money, medals, and related objects. Numismatic material means items that people collect because of their historical or monetary value. Second, the section changes the rules for bringing numismatic materials into the country. Under the new rules, someone bringing in numismatic materials must show satisfactory evidence that the material was acquired lawfully, is of a known type, and is not known to be the direct product of illicit excavations within a State Party. A State Party means a country that has agreed to follow the same international rules about cultural property. Third, the section explains what counts as satisfactory evidence for numismatic materials. The person bringing in the items must make one or more sworn statements. These statements must say that to the best of their knowledge, the numismatic material was acquired lawfully in one or more State Parties, was lawfully exported from a State Party where it was acquired, is of a type known to exist in multiple examples which has been published in a reference work on numismatics, and is not known to be the direct product of illicit excavations within another State Party after import restrictions began for that country. Finally, the section says that customs officers cannot require any additional documentation beyond what is specifically required unless the customs officer has probable cause based on documentary evidence to believe that the satisfactory evidence is false or fraudulent. Probable cause means a reasonable belief based on facts that a crime may have been committed.
